card.title: Why Time Zones Are So Weird
card.teaser: Why is all of China one time zone — and Nepal 45 minutes off from everyone?
card.coverImagePrompt: A vintage world map overlaid with twisted, irregular time zone boundaries and clock faces at different hours scattered across continents, cinematic and chaotic

lessons.title: Two cities, same longitude, different days
lessons.body: It's noon in Kashgar, in western China. 3,200 kilometers east in Shanghai, it's also noon — same time, by law. But Kashgar is closer to Tehran's longitude than Beijing's, so the sun there actually peaks around 3 p.m. Meanwhile, Nepal sets its clocks 5 hours and 45 minutes ahead of UTC — a deliberately odd offset that puts it 15 minutes away from India. Time, it turns out, is political.

visual.imagePrompt: A stylized world map showing China as a single color despite spanning multiple sun-angles, with small clock faces pinned to major cities

lessons.title: Before time zones, chaos
lessons.body: Until the 1800s, every town set its clocks by local noon — whenever the sun hit its highest point. Boston's noon was 12 minutes behind New York's. A single U.S. train trip could cross 100+ different 'times.' Railroads couldn't publish workable schedules, and head-on collisions happened because conductors disagreed about what time it was. Clocks weren't broken — the world just hadn't agreed to synchronize.

keyTerms.definition: The time measured by the actual position of the sun overhead
An agreed-upon time shared across a region, independent of local solar noon

visual.imagePrompt: A 19th-century steam train station with a wall covered in dozens of clocks, each showing a different time, sepia tones

lessons.title: How we carved up the planet
lessons.body: In 1884, delegates from 25 nations met at the International Meridian Conference in Washington, D.C. They picked the observatory in Greenwich, England as longitude zero and divided the globe into 24 one-hour zones, each 15° wide. Neat in theory. In practice, countries drew their own zone lines along borders, mountain ranges, and political alliances — which is why the map looks nothing like clean vertical stripes.

visual.imagePrompt: A map of the world from 1884 with idealized 15-degree time zone stripes, annotated in fine pen

lessons.title: The weirdest zones on Earth
lessons.body: China spans 5 solar time zones but uses only one — Beijing Time — a 1949 Mao-era decision meant to symbolize national unity. India sits on a single 30-minute offset (UTC+5:30). Nepal offsets by 5:45 specifically to differentiate from India. Venezuela moved to UTC−4:30 in 2007 purely for Hugo Chávez's political signaling, then reverted in 2016. And some Antarctic research stations technically share a time zone with their supply country, not their longitude.
lessons.funFact: The line dividing Kiribati's time zones was redrawn in 1994 so the whole country could celebrate the year 2000 first — moving the International Date Line by thousands of miles.

visual.imagePrompt: A close-up map of the strange international date line zigzagging through the Pacific Ocean, annotated

lessons.title: Time is a story we tell
lessons.body: The hour on your phone isn't measured from the sun — it's voted on by governments. Every border, offset, and daylight-saving rule is a human decision, often political, sometimes arbitrary, occasionally petty. The clock is one of civilization's most underrated diplomatic achievements. It also means if you ever feel jet-lagged, you're not confused — you're just out of sync with a political agreement.

quiz.question: How many official time zones does China use?
quiz.explanation: Despite spanning roughly 5 solar hours of sunlight, China has used a single time zone (Beijing Time) since 1949.

quiz.question: What 1884 event established the modern world time zone system?
options: The International Meridian Conference in Washington D.C.
quiz.explanation: 25 nations agreed on Greenwich as longitude zero and 24 one-hour zones.

quiz.question: Why does Nepal use the unusual offset of UTC+5:45?
options: To align with solar noon in Kathmandu
Because of a historical clock-making error
Specifically to be different from India's UTC+5:30
quiz.explanation: Nepal chose a 15-minute offset from India as a deliberate mark of independence.

sources.title: Time Lord: Sir Sandford Fleming and the Creation of Standard Time
